does: This paper addresses sparse-reward multi-agent reinforcement learning by proposing a framework that combines online inverse preference learning with multi-agent on-policy optimization. The core contribution is an implicit multi-agent reward learning model built on a preference-based value-decomposition network that generates both global and local reward signals. These signals construct dual advantage streams to enable differentiated learning targets for centralized critics and decentralized actors. The method also incorporates large language models to provide preference labels that improve the learned reward model's quality. By bridging the gap between sparse terminal rewards and dense learning signals, the approach enables effective policy optimization in multi-agent settings where standard algorithms struggle due to insufficient intermediate feedback.
connects: This work builds on several established research directions: inverse reinforcement learning and reward learning from preferences, which have been developed to infer reward functions from demonstrations or comparisons; multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithms that use centralized-training-decentralized-execution architectures and value decomposition for credit assignment; and sparse-reward RL methods that address the challenge of learning from infrequent feedback signals. The paper extends these lines by specifically combining preference-based reward inference with multi-agent credit assignment through value decomposition. It also bridges MARL with recent work on using large language models as sources of structured knowledge or preference signals, responding to growing interest in leveraging LLMs to guide RL systems.
enables: This framework opens several future research directions. The integration of preference learning with MARL could enable better sample efficiency in communication-constrained or long-horizon multi-agent tasks where rewards are inherently sparse. The use of LLMs to generate preference labels suggests potential for combining structured world knowledge with multi-agent learning, enabling agents to incorporate semantic understanding of task objectives. Future work could explore extending this approach to more complex cooperative settings, non-stationary environments, or scenarios with heterogeneous reward structures across agents. The dual-stream advantage architecture may also inspire new credit assignment mechanisms in hierarchical or partially observable multi-agent systems.
